summary_text:
S:
The patient reports feeling fatigued often during the day and experiencing feverishness with chills at times. He also reports experiencing headaches and wheezing. He notes feeling cold easily and reports chills. The patient reports abdominal pain. He reports no tenderness to palpation. The patient reports a history of sedation issues. The patient reports feeling anxious and depressed, which have been worsening lately. The patient reports feeling cold easily.

O:
Physical exam reveals blood pressure to be mildly low. Heart rate is within normal limits. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. No tenderness to palpation is noted.

A:
The patient's symptoms are consistent with anemia and possible infection. The doctor suspects internal bleeding. A referral to a hematologist has been sent.

P:
The doctor orders an upper endoscopy and colonoscopy. A referral to a hematologist has been sent. The patient will start taking iron pills with orange juice. The patient should take ferrous sulfate. The doctor plans a capsule endoscopy. Blood work will be repeated in 12 weeks. The doctor orders a colonoscopy.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Iron deficiency anemia.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mrs. Christina Cooper is a 65-year-old female who presents with a long-history of iron deficiency anemia.

The patient reports she has been living with anemia since she was 13 years old. She attempted to donate blood on 12/2019 but was unable to due to her anemia. She has a history of heavy menstruation and a hysterectomy in 1996. She completed a colonoscopy 5-6 years ago with nonsignificant findings. She denies having a pacemaker, defibrillator, sleep apnea, or using oxygen.

Mrs. Cooper reports a myriad of symptoms, stating she feels fatigued often during the day, feverish with chills at times, and during activity feels she cannot catch her breath and some wheezing is present. She has been having some headaches which is uncommon for her. Additionally, she is noticing tingling sensations, cold sensitivity, and her anxiety and depression have worsened.

PAST HISTORY

Surgical
Hysterectomy, 1996.

Procedures
Colonoscopy 5-6 years ago.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Drink’s alcohol 1-2 times per year.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Ferrous Sulfate 25 mg tablet twice per day (1 in the morning, 1 in the evening), Vitamin B12 OTC.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Gastrointestinal
Abdomen non-tender.

ASSESSMENT

• Iron deficiency anemia

Mrs. Cooper is a 65-year-old female who was diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia in 12/2019 and was unable to donate blood. Her follow-up blood work in 01/2020 revealed a low hemoglobin, stable hematocrit, and normal iron labs, although, ferritin was low. She has been taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg by mouth.

PLAN

I have asked her to continue each ferrous sulfate dose with vitamin C found in orange juice for the next 12 weeks then recheck CBC, iron ferritin, B-12, and folate. A referral was sent for her to see a hematologist. We will plan for EGD and colonoscopy to assess for potential sources of anemia or GI bleed. If this is inconclusive, capsule endoscopy can be considered.

INSTRUCTIONS

Schedule upper endoscopy, colonoscopy and appointment with hematologist. Return to the clinic if symptoms worsen.

sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports feeling fatigued often during the day and experiencing feverishness with chills at times. He also reports experiencing headaches and wheezing. He notes feeling cold easily and reports chills. The patient reports abdominal pain. He reports no tenderness to palpation. The patient reports a history of sedation issues. sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: Physical exam reveals blood pressure to be mildly low. Heart rate is within normal limits. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. No tenderness to palpation is noted.

sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The patient's symptoms are consistent with anemia and possible infection. The doctor suspects internal bleeding. A referral to a hematologist has been sent.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: The doctor orders an upper endoscopy and colonoscopy. A referral to a hematologist has been sent. The patient will start taking iron pills with orange juice. The patient should take ferrous sulfate. The doctor plans a capsule endoscopy. Blood work will be repeated in 12 weeks. The doctor orders a colonoscopy.

section_miscategorizations.text: He reports no tenderness to palpation.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Tenderness to palpation is a physical exam finding; it is determined by clinician examination, not typically by patient report. Placing it in S could confuse whether this was actually examined.

section_miscategorizations.text: A referral to a hematologist has been sent.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Referrals are management actions and belong in the Plan section, not in the Assessment. Including it in A blurs the distinction between diagnostic impression and treatment plan.
